standard.title: WebRTC: Real-Time Communication in Browsers (W3C Editor's Draft)

summary.applicability_rationale: The web-conference application directly uses the RTCPeerConnection API, getUserMedia, and related WebRTC APIs defined in this specification. The application's JavaScript code in room.js creates RTCPeerConnection instances, manages offers/answers, handles ICE candidates, and controls media tracks. While the browser implements the spec, the application's usage patterns and configuration choices directly affect compliance.

findings.requirement: RTCConfiguration iceServers: An array of objects describing servers available to be used by ICE, such as STUN and TURN servers.

evidence.observation: The application provides a valid RTCConfiguration with iceServers containing two STUN servers using the correct 'urls' property format. However, no TURN servers are configured, which means connectivity will fail in network scenarios where direct peer-to-peer and STUN-based server-reflexive candidates cannot establish a path (e.g., symmetric NATs on both sides).
evidence.gap_description: No TURN servers are configured. While the spec does not mandate TURN servers, the Editor's Draft Section 4.2.1 and the operational guidance in RFC 8825 strongly recommend TURN for reliable connectivity. The absence of TURN servers limits NAT traversal capability.

findings.remediation: Add at least one TURN server to the iceServers configuration. Consider using a service like Twilio, Xirsys, or deploying coturn for self-hosted TURN. Example: { urls: 'turn:turn.example.com:443?transport=tcp', username: 'user', credential: 'pass' }

findings.requirement: RTCIceServer urls property is required and must be a USVString or sequence of USVStrings representing STUN or TURN URIs.

source_files.code_snippet:
{ urls: 'stun:stun.l.google.com:19302' },
{ urls: 'stun:stun1.l.google.com:19302' }

evidence.observation: The application correctly uses the 'urls' property (not the deprecated 'url' property) with valid STUN URIs conforming to RFC 7064 format.

findings.requirement: The iceTransportPolicy defaults to 'all' indicating which candidates the ICE Agent is allowed to use.

evidence.observation: No explicit iceTransportPolicy is set, which means the browser default of 'all' is used. This is the recommended default per the spec.

findings.requirement: The bundlePolicy defaults to 'balanced', indicating which media-bundling policy to use when gathering ICE candidates.

source_files.code_snippet: this.iceServers = { iceServers: [...] };

evidence.observation: No explicit bundlePolicy is set, so the browser default of 'balanced' is used, which is appropriate for this application.

findings.requirement: When the RTCPeerConnection constructor is invoked, the user agent MUST run the specified steps including initialization of signaling state to 'stable', ICE connection state to 'new', etc.

source_files.code_snippet: const connection = new RTCPeerConnection(this.iceServers);

evidence.observation: The application correctly constructs RTCPeerConnection with a valid RTCConfiguration object. The browser handles internal initialization per the spec. The constructor is invoked once per peer in createPeerConnection().

findings.requirement: As one of the unloading document cleanup steps, for each RTCPeerConnection object connection whose relevant global object is window, close the connection.

evidence.observation: The browser handles the unloading cleanup step automatically per spec. However, the application only explicitly closes connections when the user clicks 'Leave'. There is no beforeunload/unload event handler to ensure clean disconnection if the user closes the tab or navigates away. While the browser will close connections per spec, the server-side signaling (socket.io) may not get a clean disconnect signal.
evidence.gap_description: No beforeunload event handler is registered to ensure graceful cleanup of peer connections and socket when the browser tab is closed or user navigates away unexpectedly.

findings.remediation: Add a window.addEventListener('beforeunload', ...) handler that calls leaveRoom() or at minimum disconnects the socket and closes peer connections.

findings.requirement: The createOffer method generates an SDP offer with supported configurations for the session. Session descriptions generated by createOffer MUST be immediately usable by setLocalDescription.

evidence.observation: The application correctly calls createOffer() with no options, then immediately passes the result to setLocalDescription(). The sequence createOffer() -> setLocalDescription(offer) follows the spec exactly. The offer is then sent via the signaling channel.

findings.requirement: The createAnswer method generates an SDP answer compatible with the supplied remote description. setRemoteDescription MUST have been called prior to calling createAnswer.

evidence.observation: The application correctly calls setRemoteDescription(offer) before createAnswer(), then setLocalDescription(answer). This follows the JSEP answer flow: setRemoteDescription(offer) -> createAnswer() -> setLocalDescription(answer) -> send answer. The sequence is correct.

findings.requirement: RTCSessionDescription objects are used with setLocalDescription and setRemoteDescription. The Editor's Draft notes that explicit RTCSessionDescription construction is no longer necessary as setLocalDescription/setRemoteDescription accept RTCSessionDescriptionInit directly.

source_files.code_snippet: await peer.connection.setRemoteDescription(new RTCSessionDescription(offer));
await peer.connection.setRemoteDescription(new RTCSessionDescription(answer));

evidence.observation: The application uses 'new RTCSessionDescription(offer)' and 'new RTCSessionDescription(answer)' when calling setRemoteDescription. The Editor's Draft and modern browsers accept RTCSessionDescriptionInit objects directly (plain objects with type and sdp). While the explicit constructor usage is not incorrect (it still works), it is considered a deprecated/legacy pattern. The createOffer/createAnswer results are passed directly to setLocalDescription (which is the modern pattern), but setRemoteDescription still uses the explicit constructor.
evidence.gap_description: Uses deprecated RTCSessionDescription constructor for setRemoteDescription calls. Modern practice (and the Editor's Draft recommendation) is to pass the plain object directly.

findings.remediation: Replace 'new RTCSessionDescription(offer)' with just 'offer' and 'new RTCSessionDescription(answer)' with just 'answer' in the setRemoteDescription calls.

findings.requirement: The set session description algorithm processes offers and answers according to the signaling state machine, transitioning through stable -> have-local-offer -> stable (for offerer) and stable -> have-remote-offer -> stable (for answerer).

source_files.code_snippet:
const offer = await connection.createOffer();
await connection.setLocalDescription(offer);

evidence.observation: The application follows the correct state machine transitions: Offerer: stable -> setLocalDescription(offer) -> have-local-offer -> setRemoteDescription(answer) -> stable. Answerer: stable -> setRemoteDescription(offer) -> have-remote-offer -> setLocalDescription(answer) -> stable. Both paths are correctly implemented.

findings.requirement: The addIceCandidate method provides a remote candidate to the ICE Agent. If remoteDescription is not set, the candidate must be buffered.

evidence.observation: The application correctly implements ICE candidate buffering. When ICE candidates arrive before remoteDescription is set, they are buffered in this.pendingCandidates. After setRemoteDescription completes (in both the 'offer' and 'answer' handlers), flushPendingCandidates() is called to add all buffered candidates. The check 'peer.connection.remoteDescription' ensures addIceCandidate is only called when the remote description exists.

findings.requirement: The Editor's Draft notes that addIceCandidate can accept RTCIceCandidateInit objects directly without constructing RTCIceCandidate.

source_files.code_snippet: await peer.connection.addIceCandidate(new RTCIceCandidate(candidate));

evidence.observation: The application uses 'new RTCIceCandidate(candidate)' when calling addIceCandidate. Modern browsers accept plain RTCIceCandidateInit objects directly. Like RTCSessionDescription, the explicit constructor is a legacy pattern.
evidence.gap_description: Uses deprecated RTCIceCandidate constructor. Modern practice is to pass the candidate object directly.

findings.remediation: Replace 'new RTCIceCandidate(candidate)' with just 'candidate' in all addIceCandidate calls.

findings.requirement: The onicecandidate event is fired when a new ICE candidate is made available to the script, or when ICE gathering completes (null candidate).

evidence.observation: The application correctly handles the onicecandidate event and sends non-null candidates to the remote peer via the signaling server. When event.candidate is null (end of gathering), the event is silently ignored, which is acceptable behavior for trickle ICE.

findings.requirement: The ontrack event fires when a new incoming track is added to the RTCPeerConnection, providing the MediaStreamTrack and associated MediaStreams.

evidence.observation: The application correctly handles the ontrack event and uses event.streams[0] to get the associated MediaStream. The stream is then attached to a video element for display. This is the correct modern pattern (using ontrack rather than the deprecated onaddstream).

findings.requirement: The connectionstatechange event fires when the RTCPeerConnectionState changes. Applications should monitor this for disconnected/failed states.

evidence.observation: The application monitors connectionState for 'disconnected' and 'failed' states and removes the peer when either occurs. However, it does not implement any ICE restart logic when the connection transitions to 'failed', which the spec recommends. The transition to 'disconnected' may be transient, and immediately removing the peer may be too aggressive.
evidence.gap_description: No ICE restart mechanism is implemented when connectionState transitions to 'failed'. The spec recommends performing an ICE restart (createOffer with iceRestart: true) when iceConnectionState transitions to 'failed'. Also, immediate peer removal on 'disconnected' may be premature.

findings.remediation: Implement ICE restart logic when connectionState is 'failed' using createOffer({ iceRestart: true }). Consider adding a delay or timeout before removing a peer in the 'disconnected' state, as it may recover.

findings.requirement: addTrack adds a new track to the RTCPeerConnection with an associated MediaStream. It returns an RTCRtpSender.

evidence.observation: The application correctly uses addTrack() with both the track and the associated MediaStream as arguments. This associates the audio and video tracks with the same MediaStream, enabling proper lip-sync grouping in the SDP.

findings.requirement: replaceTrack() replaces the track being sent by the RTCRtpSender without renegotiation.

evidence.observation: The application correctly uses replaceTrack() for screen sharing, swapping the camera video track with the screen share track without renegotiation. This is the recommended approach per the spec for track replacement. The same pattern is used when stopping screen share to revert back to the camera track.

findings.requirement: getSenders() returns a sequence of RTCRtpSender objects representing the RTP senders currently attached to the RTCPeerConnection.

evidence.observation: The application correctly uses getSenders() to find the video sender for track replacement during screen sharing. The filter by s.track.kind === 'video' correctly identifies the video sender.

findings.requirement: The close() method closes the RTCPeerConnection, stopping all media and ICE operations.

evidence.observation: The application correctly calls close() on peer connections both when removing individual peers and when leaving the room. This properly stops all ICE, DTLS, and media operations.

findings.requirement: getUserMedia returns a promise that resolves with a MediaStream with the requested media types (audio/video). Applications MUST handle permission denied and other errors.

evidence.observation: The application correctly uses navigator.mediaDevices.getUserMedia() with proper constraints. It implements a graceful fallback pattern: first attempts audio+video, and if that fails (e.g., no camera), falls back to audio-only. Error handling is in place at both levels.

findings.requirement: Setting the enabled property of a MediaStreamTrack to false causes the track to produce silence (audio) or black frames (video), effectively muting/disabling the track.

evidence.observation: The application correctly uses the track.enabled property to mute/unmute audio and disable/enable video. This is the proper approach per the spec for local muting without renegotiation.

findings.requirement: The stop() method permanently stops a MediaStreamTrack, releasing associated resources.

evidence.observation: The application correctly calls stop() on all tracks when leaving the room and when stopping screen sharing. This properly releases camera, microphone, and screen capture resources.

findings.requirement: getDisplayMedia() requests the user to select a display surface to capture. The returned MediaStream contains video tracks.

evidence.observation: The application correctly uses getDisplayMedia with video constraint. It handles the track's onended event (fired when the user stops sharing via the browser's native UI) to properly revert to camera. The replaceTrack pattern is used to switch video without renegotiation.

findings.requirement: RTCDataChannel provides a generic transport service allowing web browsers to exchange generic data in a peer-to-peer fashion.

evidence.observation: The application does not use RTCDataChannel. Chat messaging is implemented via the Socket.IO signaling server rather than peer-to-peer data channels. This is a valid architectural choice and does not represent a compliance issue, as data channels are optional.

findings.requirement: RTCRtpTransceiver represents a combination of an RTCRtpSender and RTCRtpReceiver. The application may use addTransceiver or addTrack to create transceivers.

evidence.observation: The application implicitly creates transceivers through addTrack(), which is a valid approach. The browser manages the transceiver lifecycle correctly. No explicit addTransceiver() calls are needed for this application's use case.

findings.requirement: The negotiationneeded event fires when a change has occurred which requires session negotiation. The application should create a new offer in response.

evidence.observation: The application does not register a handler for the 'negotiationneeded' event on the RTCPeerConnection. Instead, it manually manages when to create offers based on the initiator flag. While this works for the initial connection setup, it means that any subsequent changes that would normally trigger renegotiation (e.g., adding/removing tracks after the connection is established) will not automatically trigger a new offer/answer exchange.
evidence.gap_description: No onnegotiationneeded handler is registered. The application relies on manual offer creation which does not handle mid-session renegotiation scenarios.

findings.remediation: Add a connection.onnegotiationneeded handler that creates and sends a new offer when the browser determines renegotiation is needed. Implement proper handling for signaling glare (both sides creating offers simultaneously).

findings.requirement: The icegatheringstatechange event fires when the ICE gathering state changes, transitioning between 'new', 'gathering', and 'complete'.

evidence.observation: The application does not monitor ICE gathering state changes. This is not required by the spec. The application uses trickle ICE by sending candidates as they arrive via onicecandidate, which is a valid approach that doesn't require monitoring gathering state.

findings.requirement: The getStats() method gathers statistics for the RTCPeerConnection and its associated streams.

evidence.observation: The application does not use getStats() for monitoring connection quality. While not required, implementing getStats() would enable the application to monitor connection health, detect quality issues, and make informed decisions about ICE restarts or quality adaptation.

findings.requirement: The spec notes that signaling is provided by unspecified means, but generally by a script via the server. The signaling channel must reliably relay offers, answers, and ICE candidates between peers.

evidence.observation: The server correctly relays offers, answers, and ICE candidates between peers using Socket.IO as the signaling transport. Each message is forwarded to the specific target peer using socket.to(to).emit(). The server does not modify or inspect the SDP/candidate content, acting as a transparent relay.

findings.requirement: WebRTC APIs including getUserMedia and RTCPeerConnection require a secure context (HTTPS or localhost). The application MUST be served over HTTPS in production.

source_files.code_snippet:
const app = express();
const server = http.createServer(app);

services:
  - type: web
    name: web-conference
    runtime: node
    buildCommand: npm install
    startCommand: node server/index.js
    envVars:
      - key: NODE_ENV
        value: production

evidence.observation: The server uses plain HTTP (http.createServer). While the render.yaml deployment configuration suggests deploying on Render.com which provides HTTPS via its reverse proxy, the application itself has no HTTPS configuration. For local development, localhost is a secure context, but any other non-HTTPS deployment would break getUserMedia and RTCPeerConnection.
evidence.gap_description: The server creates an HTTP server only. No HTTPS configuration is present. WebRTC APIs require secure contexts. The application relies on the deployment platform (Render) to provide HTTPS termination.

findings.remediation: Either add HTTPS support directly to the Node.js server for self-hosted deployments, or document that HTTPS is required and must be provided by the deployment platform or a reverse proxy like nginx.

recommendations.title: Add TURN server configuration for reliable connectivity
recommendations.description: The application only configures STUN servers, which will fail to establish connections in many real-world network conditions (symmetric NATs, enterprise firewalls). Add TURN server(s) to the iceServers configuration to ensure connectivity in all scenarios. Consider making TURN server configuration environment-variable driven.

recommendations.title: Implement onnegotiationneeded handler for renegotiation support
recommendations.description: Add a handler for the 'negotiationneeded' event to support dynamic session changes (e.g., adding/removing tracks during a call). This is essential for features like adding screen sharing as a separate track rather than replacing the camera track. Also implement glare handling for simultaneous offers.

recommendations.title: Implement ICE restart on connection failure
recommendations.description: When the connection state transitions to 'failed', the application should attempt an ICE restart by calling createOffer({ iceRestart: true }) rather than immediately removing the peer. Also consider adding a delay for the 'disconnected' state before removing the peer.

recommendations.title: Remove deprecated constructor usage
recommendations.description: Replace 'new RTCSessionDescription(...)' and 'new RTCIceCandidate(...)' with direct object passing. Modern browsers and the Editor's Draft recommend passing RTCSessionDescriptionInit and RTCIceCandidateInit objects directly to setRemoteDescription() and addIceCandidate() respectively.

recommendations.title: Add HTTPS support or document HTTPS requirements
recommendations.description: WebRTC requires secure contexts. The application should either implement HTTPS directly or clearly document that deployment behind an HTTPS-terminating proxy is required. Consider adding an HTTPS option for self-hosted deployments.

recommendations.title: Add beforeunload handler for graceful cleanup
recommendations.description: Register a window.addEventListener('beforeunload', ...) handler to ensure peer connections and the signaling socket are properly closed when the user closes the tab or navigates away without clicking 'Leave'.
